pattern: Sort the prices, then binary search the answer: a candidate minimum gap is achievable exactly when a greedy left-to-right pick, always taking the next price at least that far above the last pick, selects at least k prices.
hints: Sort the prices first -- the best possible chosen subset, viewed in sorted order, has its tightest gap between two consecutive chosen elements, never two far-apart ones.
Binary search on the candidate tastiness value d and check feasibility by greedily walking the sorted prices, taking one whenever it is at least d past the last one taken.
If greedily taking prices at least d apart yields k or more items, d is achievable -- binary search for the largest such d.

edge_inputs.description: k = 2 always just needs the two most extreme prices
k equals the full basket size, so every price must be used
duplicate prices in the middle tempt a greedy picker into a worse triple
a cluster of identical prices sits next to one outlier
the smallest possible basket of two identical prices
one far outlier and two close prices

stress.description: a large random price list with k anywhere from 2 up to the basket size
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 100000))
    price = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(2, n)
    return [price, k]
